metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either qualify the shell pattern as POSIX/bash-only with a note that Windows users need an equivalent PowerShell idiom, or provide a cross-platform alternative (e.g., a small Python snippet using `subprocess`/`git credential`) so the guidance is valid across all supported platforms.
evidence: Plan text: 'Provide a shell pattern that keeps credentials in variables, passes them directly to curl, and never echoes or logs the password.'
The standard idiom for `git credential fill` input (a heredoc or echo pipe) and command substitution `VAR=$(...)` is POSIX-shell-only syntax.
Support matrix explicitly lists Windows as a supported platform and venue.
Windows cmd.exe has no `$(...)` substitution; PowerShell syntax differs materially (e.g., `$(git credential fill ...)` is not directly composable with curl in the same way).
findings.finding: The plan calls for a POSIX shell pattern (variable assignment via command substitution, piping to `git credential fill`, passing credentials to `curl`) in AGENTS.md, but this syntax is incompatible with Windows cmd.exe and PowerShell. A developer or Codex session running on Windows following the documented guidance would encounter immediate shell syntax failures.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Provide a shell pattern that keeps credentials in variables, passes them directly to curl, and never echoes or logs the password.'
scenarios: Windows platform (developer workstation or CI on Windows): a developer or Codex session following the documented shell pattern would see `syntax error` or `'$' is not recognized` when executing the credential-acquisition snippet, making the guidance non-functional on that platform.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical in AGENTS.md noting '(bash/POSIX shell; on Windows use PowerShell `$creds = git credential fill ... | ...` or a Python equivalent)' and update the test assertion to check for this qualifier.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence in the Implementation section explaining why the credential guidance must remain inline in the 'Codex Gerrit workflow rule' (e.g. the rule is already an inline exception to the lean-file policy because it controls Codex runtime behavior, not a topic with a pre-existing docs/ home).
evidence: Plan: 'Edit the Codex Gerrit workflow rule in AGENTS.md because Codex loads that file at session start' — rationale covers WHY AGENTS.md, but not WHY inline vs. a docs/ pointer.
AGENTS.md header: 'Keep this file lean: an @-import loads at launch and does not reduce context, so already-documented topics are one-line pointers into docs/, not restated sections.'
ABSENCE: no sentence in the plan explains why the credential guidance is categorised as inline Gerrit-rule content rather than a docs/-delegated topic.
findings.finding: The plan gives a positive rationale for editing AGENTS.md (Codex loads it at session start) but does not state why the new credential content belongs inline in the Gerrit rule rather than in a docs/ sub-page with a one-line pointer — the approach AGENTS.md itself prescribes for 'already-documented topics'. Without this rationale, an implementer may reasonably relocate the content to docs/ (breaking the test's anchor in the Gerrit rule span and weakening the at-session-start guarantee).
findings.location: Implementation section, first bullet ('Edit the Codex Gerrit workflow rule in AGENTS.md')
scenarios: An implementer reads the AGENTS.md 'keep it lean' policy and moves the credential pattern to docs/gerrit-credential-guide.md with a one-line pointer, causing the test_codex_agent_guidance.py assertions (which anchor to the Gerrit rule span) to fail and potentially weakening runtime visibility.
A future plan reviewer or contributor trims the inline content to a docs/ pointer without realising that breaks the documented test boundary.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the first bullet: 'The Gerrit workflow rule is an intentional inline exception to AGENTS.md's lean-file policy: unlike documented-elsewhere topics, it governs Codex's own runtime behavior during a session and must be immediately visible without a follow-on file read.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reference `infra/gerrit/feature-branch-e2e.sh` (lines 99–114) as the existing canonical prior art for the `git credential fill` + `curl -u` shell pattern, and ensure the AGENTS.md snippet is derived from (or explicitly consistent with) that established idiom rather than introduced as a new, independent formulation.
evidence: infra/gerrit/feature-branch-e2e.sh lines 99–114: exact `git credential fill | sed` + `curl -u "$USER:$PW"` pattern with 'NEVER echoed' comment already committed
infra/gerrit/feature-branch-inventory.sh lines 39–44: same pattern, independently implemented
Plan says 'Provide a shell pattern' with no acknowledgment that this pattern already exists in the repo
findings.finding: The plan proposes documenting a 'shell pattern' for git credential fill + curl in AGENTS.md without referencing the already-implemented, tested, and commented canonical version of this exact pattern in the repo's own infra scripts (feature-branch-e2e.sh lines 99–114, feature-branch-inventory.sh lines 39–44). The plan risks producing a divergent or redundant formulation instead of pointing AGENTS.md readers to the live prior art.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Provide a shell pattern that keeps credentials in variables, passes them directly to curl, and never echoes or logs the password.'
scenarios: Codex implementing the plan authors a new shell snippet that subtly differs from the repo's established idiom (e.g., different variable naming, missing the XSSI-strip note, or missing the URL-reserved-chars caveat already documented in feature-branch-e2e.sh line 104–106)
AGENTS.md documentation drifts from the infra scripts over time because the plan established no link between them
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section, add: 'Base the AGENTS.md shell pattern directly on the existing idiom in `infra/gerrit/feature-branch-e2e.sh` (the `_CRED`/`GERRIT_PW` block and `api_get` helper), including its URL-reserved-chars caveat. Optionally, cross-link from AGENTS.md to that script so readers can inspect a live, CI-tested implementation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Require the AGENTS.md shell pattern to include an explicit guard (e.g., `|| { echo 'credential fill failed — no helper configured?' >&2; exit 1; }`) after the `git credential fill` command substitution, consistent with the existing infra scripts.
evidence: infra/gerrit/feature-branch-e2e.sh line 101: `| git credential fill 2>/dev/null) || harness "git credential fill failed"` — error-exit guard is present in the reference implementation but the plan does not require it in the AGENTS.md pattern
infra/gerrit/feature-branch-inventory.sh line 40: same guard pattern `|| { echo 'credential fill failed' >&2; exit 2; }`
Plan says 'passes them directly to curl' but does not specify a guard for empty/failed fill output
Partially-verified: the happy-path integration is Verified; the failure-path handling is Unverified for the documented pattern
findings.finding: The plan's shell pattern passes credentials obtained via `git credential fill` directly to `curl -u`, but does not state the precondition that a Git credential helper must be configured — if no helper is set, `git credential fill` silently yields empty output and the curl call will still be made with an empty password, producing a 401. The existing infra scripts handle this with an explicit error-exit guard (`|| harness ...`), but the plan does not require the AGENTS.md documentation to include a failure-path note, leaving the Codex reader without the guard pattern.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Provide a shell pattern that keeps credentials in variables, passes them directly to curl'
scenarios: Codex runs in an environment where no Git credential helper is configured; fill succeeds but returns no password; curl sends an empty-password request and gets a 401; Codex cannot distinguish this from the very credential-invalid false-positive the plan aims to prevent.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to the AGENTS.md pattern: `CRED="$(printf 'protocol=https
host=%s
username=%s

' "$HOST" "$USER" | git credential fill 2>/dev/null)" || { echo 'git credential fill failed — is a credential helper configured?' >&2; exit 1; }` — mirrors the guard already present in infra/gerrit/feature-branch-e2e.sh.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the AGENTS.md shell pattern (and the corresponding AC) that the Gerrit password must NOT appear as a command-line argument to curl (e.g., use `--netrc-file <tmpfile>` with a `trap`-cleaned temp file, or pipe credentials via stdin with `--user @-`), so the secret is absent from process listings as well as logs.
evidence: Plan text: 'passes them directly to curl, and never echoes or logs the password' — the mechanism for passing to curl is unspecified.
If `curl --user "$USER:$PASS"` is used, the password appears in `ps aux` output, violating the spirit of the no-exposure requirement even though it satisfies the literal no-echo/no-log constraint.
ABSENCE: The plan does not specify use of `--netrc-file <tmpfile>`, `curl --config -` / stdin injection, or any other mechanism that keeps the password out of the process argument list.
findings.finding: The plan commits to 'never echoes or logs the password' but does not specify how credentials are passed to curl. If the documented shell pattern passes the password as a visible command-line argument (e.g., `curl --user "$USER:$PASS" ...`), the secret is exposed in process listings (`ps aux`) to other local users — a local trust-boundary crossing the T5c gate opens at reduced blast radius.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Provide a shell pattern that keeps credentials in variables, passes them directly to curl, and never echoes or logs the password.'
scenarios: On a shared developer machine or CI runner, another local user or process running `ps aux` at the right moment reads the Gerrit password from the curl command-line argument.
A process-monitoring tool or audit log captures the full argv of every subprocess, inadvertently recording the Gerrit password.
findings.suggested_fix:
Document a pattern such as:
```sh
creds=$(git credential fill <<EOF
protocol=https
host=<gerrit-host>
EOF
)
user=$(printf '%s' "$creds" | sed -n 's/^username=//p')
pass=$(printf '%s' "$creds" | sed -n 's/^password=//p')
tmprc=$(mktemp)
trap 'rm -f "$tmprc"' EXIT
printf 'user = %s:%s\n' "$user" "$pass" > "$tmprc"
curl --netrc-file "$tmprc" https://<gerrit-host>/a/...
```
This keeps the password out of the process argument list while satisfying the no-echo/no-log requirement already stated in the plan.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
